title: Ribollita
subtitle: The Tuscan bread and bean soup that thickens overnight
excerpt: Cannellini beans, cavolo nero, and country bread simmered into a thick Tuscan soup that is reheated the next day ,  which is when it is at its best. One of the most satisfying cold-weather soups in the Italian tradition.
sourceNotes: Ribollita (meaning 'reboiled') is a Tuscan peasant soup traditionally made by reheating leftover minestrone with stale bread. This version follows the standard modern approach with cannellini beans and cavolo nero.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze before adding the bread. The soup base freezes well for up to three months. Add fresh bread when reheating.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The name means 'reboiled': it is designed to be made a day ahead and reheated. It is noticeably better the second day.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dark-leafed Italian kale, also called Tuscan kale or black cabbage. The leaves are longer and darker than curly kale and have a less bitter, earthier flavour. It holds up well to long cooking, making it ideal for soups like ribollita.

content.text: Ribollita means reboiled: it is made to be eaten the next day. The bread added at the end of the first cooking absorbs the broth overnight and transforms the soup into something thick, almost porridge-like, that only needs a hard reheat and a generous pour of olive oil to be ready.
is the traditional green; curly kale or Savoy cabbage work if cavolo nero is not available. Use stale bread ,  the older and drier the better, since it absorbs more broth.

items.prepNote: rind reserved for cooking, finely grated to serve

content.text: Heat {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil in a large heavy pot over a medium-low heat. Add the {{onion}} onion, {{carrot}} carrots, and {{celery}} celery. Cook for 12 minutes until very soft. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic, {{thyme-dried}} of thyme, and {{rosemary}} of rosemary and cook for 2 minutes.
Add {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes, crushing them with the back of a spoon. If using a Parmesan rind, add it now. Simmer for 10 minutes.

Add the {{cannellini-beans}} of drained cannellini beans and pour in {{stock-vegetable}} of hot stock. Bring to a simmer. Use the back of a spoon to roughly crush about a quarter of the beans against the side of the pot to thicken the broth.
Add the {{cavolo-nero}} of torn cavolo nero leaves. Simmer for 20 minutes until the greens are very tender.

Remove the Parmesan rind and rosemary sprig. Stir in {{bread}} of torn stale bread. Press the bread down into the soup so it absorbs the broth. Take off the heat and leave to rest for at least 1 hour, or refrigerate overnight.

To serve, reheat gently, stirring as the bread breaks down into the soup. Thin with a little water if it has become too thick. Taste and season with salt. Ladle into bowls and drizzle generously with olive oil. Finish with grated Parmesan if using.

items.symptom: Soup is too thick to ladle
items.cause: The bread has absorbed a very large amount of broth overnight.
items.fix: Add hot water or stock when reheating, stirring as it warms. Ribollita is meant to be thick but it should still move in the pot.

items.symptom: Bread has made the soup lumpy
items.cause: The bread was added in large pieces or was too fresh and has become gluey rather than dissolving.
items.fix: Use stale bread, torn into small chunks rather than sliced. Press it down into the soup firmly. The lumpy stage is temporary; the second reheat smooths it out.

items.cause: Cavolo nero needs longer cooking than most cabbage varieties.
items.fix: Give it the full 20 minutes of simmering. It should be dark, completely soft, and almost melting into the broth.

items.symptom: Flavour is flat on the first day
items.cause: Ribollita improves overnight as the bread and beans continue to thicken the broth and the flavours meld.
items.fix: Taste again on the second day before declaring it bland. A generous pour of good olive oil on each bowl at serving lifts the whole soup significantly.

content.text: Ribollita is Tuscan peasant food made from the most basic ingredients: beans, greens, stale bread, and olive oil. The name comes directly from the method ,  reboiled ,  because leftover minestrone was historically reheated the next day with bread added to make it more filling. Modern ribollita is made from scratch with this same intention: a soup designed not to be eaten fresh but to improve with a night's rest. It is a dish that rewards patience and a good bottle of olive oil.
